item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I got a real chuckle out of this, surprised it's not a j. But good enough for the main list.

Thanks for the fun article

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to believe that this is real.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Using a bot to transcribe makes it LESS likely that the lisp would appear in the transcript, not more. Bots don't just map each individual sound to the closest letter(s); that would likely result in sum theeng dif ik ult tu reed at best, even when used on someone with perfect enunciation. They try to find collections of sounds that match most closely to actual words, and then use those words. Speech impediments and heavy accents ARE difficult for machine-generated transcripts, but the consequence of those difficulties is a transcript full of perfectly-spelled word salad. It just doesn't make sense to transcribe the lisp.
Also worth noting: it's kind of offensive to people with speech impediments. I know you probably didn't intend to be malicious here, but, well…the speech impediment isn't relevant to the story, and it is part of the joke. It's pretty much just "ha ha speech impediments are funny".
People know what Mike Tyson sounds like; you can just write out his words normally and trust the reader to supply his voice properly. If you have to remind people, maybe leave a note at the beginning about how parts of the transcript were unclear due to the subject's speech impediment and are the best guesses on the part of the writers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Transcription bots most certainly do not phonetically record.

item.title: Site Crit Team
item.content:encoded:
Lisp aside, this is well written and I did find the gimmick entertaining, it falls short of really leaning into the strength of the article. It succeeds as a sort of almost vignette SCP but I kept waiting for this to really climb up another level/notch and it never did.

I would have loved a chance to see beyond this pigeon thing in some fashion other than a kind of one-liner ear-bite joke.

How does the -1 instance fly away at the end of the interview? It's a 4-meter tall bird and we were indoors for an interview?

I also find the containment note at the end to be unnecessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's not how transcription software works, not remotely. I read auto-generated transcripts at work, and there's already some incredibly sophisticated transcription software out there, but you can watch the auto-generated captions on YouTube for a better idea of what it looks like when a caption bot doesn't understand what it's hearing.

Either your transcription bot knows what word is being said, in which case you get the actual word spelled correctly, or it has no idea. If the bot doesn't know the word, some software gives a best guess at what the word probably is (and hilarity ensues), or it might spell the whole unknown phrase phonetically, syllable by syllable.

No one is going to create or use a transcription bot that can spell the entire word correctly EXCEPT for the lisp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well to each their own though I will, however, point out that I included an in-universe reason as to why his lisp was included. There are 3025 videos, way more than a person could translate in a reasonable amount of time, so due to the sheer amount of videos, the bot that traces the video automatically transcribes them verbatim. Whether that's enough of an explanation for you is beside the point, just felt I should point out that an explanation was in fact included.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the overall concept of it, and I enjoy SCPs along the lines of "such-and-such public figure actually has unexpected anomalous abilities," but the lisp is just crude. It's such a cheap joke, because there's not really any explanation for why a Foundation transcript would go out of its way to swap out every single S like that.

It really drags down the whole article, because instead of being a lighthearted bit about a celebrity with unexpected abilities, it relies on the existence of Mike Tyson being a punchline by itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was pretty sure this comment would come up about this SCP, so I've got an argument for why I personally believe that this works better as a mainlister.

Firstly, comedic mainlist SCPs can work. There are several successful ones, and they've been a thing ever since series one (SCP-297). Even comedic, obviously false SCPs about famous people exist on the mainlist (SCP-4444).

Secondly, the lisp. As there are 3025 recorded magic minutes, the Foundation turned to a bot to transcribe. This is mentioned in the SCP, and is a functional in-universe explanation for transcribing the lisp as the bot would just write down exactly what it hears.

For these two reasons, I think that while this is definitely on the sillier end, it is justified as a mainlist SCP. Of course, your opinion may differ, and that's cool with me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
you made mike tyson into an SCP.

you included his lisp in the transcripts.

you put in an ear biting joke??

+1 but are we sure this isn't a -J?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This looks like between a -J and a main list SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well here is something we can agree on, I will most like not be posting them, while writing them it seemed I was beating a dead horthe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I strongly question what longevity any extended video log will have, since they all seem to rotate around "Mike Tyson turns X into a pigeon". Nor could such a log be like the extended logs of, say, SCP-3780, featuring different creative ways to reach the same ending — since the journey and destination are both "Mike Tyson turns X into a pigeon".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was thoroughly disappointing in terms of a celebrity-centered, comedic SCP. While intruging on its face, it quickly devolves into the simplistic gimmick of "lisp, lisp, lisp, pigeon".

It seems like a missed opportunity to truly capitalize on the absurdity or surrealness of it all, ala SCPs such as SCP-2730 and SCP-4844, and I feel the piece suffers for it.

I believe it'd benefit immensely by truly leaning into its own insanity and developing the narrative a stone's throw beyond what's currently on display.

Ultimately, it's not quite creative enough for me to personally enjoy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ill try making the picture a little better, a shame you didn't enjoy but im glad you took the time to read and comment regardless, so thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Amusing, but:

  1. The image shopping isn't particularly well-done.
  2. This feels less like a humorous story and more like a slew of (admittedly funny) in-jokes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
uses its teeth to remove her left ear

This is the dumbest, best, most clinical way of saying "bites off" I have ever seen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me laugh unreasonably hard when I was critting the draft. I loved it then and I love it now, take my +1 you beautiful bastard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure what i'm feeling right now. This is the kind of thing I would see in a dream. I think that's why I really like it though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: weirdly enough, this fits in perfectly with my pre-existing image of Mike Tyson as a fundamentally absurd, but terrifying and malignant, person/thing. also, thomething thomething lithp joke. +1

item.title: Re: Thpecial Containment Prothedure
item.content:encoded: All is good.
Chrithtopher Nolan prethentth: The Pigeon Man Ritheth

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thith getth my thumbth up

item.title: Thpecial Containment Prothedure
